cards.back: It's a specialized template that doesn't behave like other vectors. It stores bits instead of bool objects, doesn't return real references, and violates expectations. It's not considered a true container.

cards.text: std::vector<bool> is a {{c1::template specialization}} that stores each bool as a single {{c2::bit}} rather than a byte to save memory.

cards.back: A proxy object (not a real bool&) that acts like a reference to a bit. This breaks code expecting a real reference.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
std::vector<bool> v{true, false};
bool& ref = v[0];
cards.answer: Won't compile. v[0] doesn't return a bool&, it returns a proxy object that can't bind to bool&.
cards.explanation: std::vector<bool> doesn't store actual bool objects, so it can't return real references.

Avoid std::vector<bool>. Better alternatives:
  • std::vector<char> or std::vector<unsigned char> - uses more memory but behaves normally
  • std::deque<bool> - similar interface, no bit-packing
  • std::bitset - for fixed-size bit arrays
  • Third-party libraries like boost::dynamic_bitset

cards.front: What is the main benefit of std::vector<bool>'s bit-packing?
cards.back: Memory efficiency - uses 1 bit per bool instead of 1 byte, reducing memory usage by 8x. However, this comes with significant behavioral problems.

cards.text: LearnCPP recommends avoiding {{c1::std::vector<bool>}} and using {{c2::std::vector<char>}} or {{c3::std::deque<bool>}} instead.

cards.front: Is std::vector<bool> considered a true container in C++?
cards.back: No. It doesn't meet all container requirements because it doesn't provide access to actual bool objects. It's a pseudo-container.

cards.question: How would you fix this code?
cards.answer:
std::vector<char> flags{true, false, true};
or
std::deque<bool> flags{true, false, true};
cards.explanation: Replace vector<bool> with vector<char> or deque<bool> to avoid the specialization issues.

cards.front: When should you use std::bitset instead of std::vector<bool>?
cards.back: When you need a fixed-size collection of bits known at compile time. std::bitset provides a clean interface for bit manipulation without the problems of vector<bool>.
